metadata.title: Binomial → Normal: The Central Limit Theorem in Action

beats.narration_text: Annyeong. A learner asks: build me an interactive simulation of binomial → normal: the central limit theorem in action. One Claude prompt. Here is what comes back.

props.command: build me an interactive sim of binomial → normal: the central limit theorem in action
output: ✓ Binomial → Normal: The Central Limit Theorem in Action

beats.narration_text: Binomial → Normal: The Central Limit Theorem in Action is one of those ideas that is easier to see than to state. the core behaviour of binomial → normal: the central limit theorem in action. This simulation makes that visible — and every educator can build one.
beats.new_visual_element: Concept card: Binomial → Normal: The Central Limit Theorem in Action

beats.narration_text: Here is the prompt. One message to Claude.
beats.code_snippet: Build a single-file interactive HTML simulation of Binomial → Normal: The Central Limit Theorem in Action. Use D3.js from CDN. All physics computed in-browser JavaScript. Sliders for: n (trials), p (probability). Verify: the key behaviour of binomial → normal: the central limit theorem in action. Palette: background #FFFFFF, ink #2A1A0E, accent #C8102E.

beats.narration_text: Claude returns a single-file HTML. Here is the heart of it — the integrator and the slider handler. No server. No build step. One file, everything in the browser.

beats.narration_text: At the default settings: binomial → normal: the central limit theorem in action. Watch what the simulation shows.
Now drag n (trials) from 2 to 200. Watch the physics respond in real time.
beats.new_visual_element: Slate: narrate slider sweep of n (trials) from 2 to 200

beats.narration_text: the prediction holds — binomial → normal: the central limit theorem in action behaves as the equations say. Exactly as the equations predict.

One prompt. A live demonstration of binomial → normal: the central limit theorem in action. For educators: every concept in your course can have one of these. Claude builds it; you judge it; your students explore it.
Open this file in your browser and drag the controls. Or ask Claude to add a second parameter, change the palette, or explain the physics step by step alongside the simulation.
Take this prompt, run it on your own topic. Your turn.
beats.code_snippet: Take this Binomial → Normal: The Central Limit Theorem in Action simulation and add a second slider that controls n (trials). Show how changing it reveals a new aspect of the physics. Keep the single-file format and D3 CDN.
